I guess it does increase their cost, or rather your share of their hardware depreciation.

children.author: staticshock
children.textRawHtml:
Think of the entirety of the context (the full thread of conversation, all tool call output, etc.) as one message that's been submitted to the LLM all at once just so it can generate the next token (which is just the next word or even syllable.) Once that token is generated it is appended to the context, and the entire context is once again used to generate the next token. Keep doing that until the entire response is generated. The larger the context, the more stuff the model has to pay attention to as it generates each next token.

To use an analogy: imagine your friend is the author of an unfinished book. They die with 19 chapters written, and on their death bed ask you to write the 20th chapter. Assuming you're up to the task, you can only do this well if you take the time to absorb the entirety of what's been written so far.

This is how LLMs work. Context caching is an optimization on top of this, but it has its limits.
children.textZhHtml:
将整个上下文（对话的完整线程、所有工具调用输出等）视为一次性提交给 LLM 的一条消息，以便它可以生成下一个标记（这只是下一个单词甚至音节。）生成该标记后，它会附加到上下文，并且整个上下文再次用于生成下一个标记。继续这样做，直到生成整个响应。上下文越大，模型在生成每个下一个标记时需要注意的内容就越多。

打个比方：想象你的朋友是一本未完成的书的作者。他们死时写下了 19 章，临终时要求你写第 20 章。假设您能够胜任这项任务，那么只有花时间吸收到目前为止所写的全部内容，您才能做好这项工作。

这就是法学硕士的工作方式。上下文缓存是对此的优化，但它有其局限性。

children.author: londons_explore
children.textRawHtml:
There are various difficulties with renting GPUs, especially if your setup is very custom.

The competitor would have to port their training systems to your specific network architecture, system design, rdma Vs ethernet vs infiniband Vs nvlink etc.

Getting it running might not be too hard, but getting it running efficiently and making good use of all those flops will require considerable human effort and wall time.

Add that to the fact most frontier labs seem to have a single huge training run - and to my knowledge nobody has figured out how to distribute that training run between data centers effectively.
children.textZhHtml:
租用 GPU 会遇到各种困难，尤其是在您的设置非常自定义的情况下。

竞争对手必须将其训练系统移植到您的特定网络架构、系统设计、rdma、以太网、infiniband、nvlink 等。

让它运行可能不会太难，但让它高效运行并充分利用所有这些失败将需要大量的人力和时间。

添加事实上，大多数前沿实验室似乎都有一次巨大的训练运行 - 据我所知，没有人知道如何在数据中心之间有效地分配该训练运行。

comments.author: minimaxir
comments.textRawHtml:
The developer's guide (https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/latest-model) has some interesting semantic tips for using the model:

> Intent understanding: GPT-5.6 can better infer the user’s underlying goal and intended level of work without you specifying every step. Continue to state important constraints, approval boundaries, and success criteria explicitly.

> Original image detail: GPT-5.6 preserves the original dimensions of images sent with original or auto detail instead of resizing them to a patch budget or pixel-dimension limit.

> Use shorter prompts: In internal evaluations, replacing long, explicit system prompts with minimal prompts improved scores by roughly 10–15%, while reducing total tokens by 41–66% and cost by 33–67%.

> Avoid generic brevity instructions: GPT-5.6 is more sensitive than GPT-5.5 to instructions such as “Be concise,” “Keep it short,” or “Use minimal text.”

> Control warmth: GPT-5.6 does not become meaningfully better when prompted to be broadly friendlier or more empathetic.
comments.textZhHtml:
开发人员指南 (https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/latest-model) 提供了一些使用该模型的有趣语义提示：

> 意图理解：GPT-5.6 可以更好地推断用户的根本目标和预期的工作级别，而无需您指定每个步骤。继续明确说明重要的约束、批准边界和成功标准。

> 原始图像细节：GPT-5.6 保留以原始或自动细节发送的图像的原始尺寸，而不是将其大小调整为补丁预算或像素尺寸限制。

> 使用较短的提示：在内部评估中，用最少的提示替换长而明确的系统提示，可将分数提高大约 10-15%，同时将总代币减少 41-66%，成本减少33–67%。

> 避免通用的简洁指令：GPT-5.6 比 GPT-5.5 对“简洁”、“保持简短”或“使用最少文本”等指令更敏感。

> 控制热情：当被提示更加友好或更富有同理心时，GPT-5.6 并不会变得明显更好。

children.author: ravenstine
children.textRawHtml:
> Avoid generic brevity instructions

That part is confusing because it's not like they provide an example of how default GPT-5.6 output compares with GPT-5.5 both with default output and prompted for brevity. Whenever I use such prompts, it's usually because I want the model to give me the gist in a few sentences. I'd be stunned if GPT-5.6 was that concise by default. I would think that could "break" a lot of things for developers who didn't know to make prompt changes after upgrading to 5.6. What if you were expecting GPT to be as wordy as it usually is? Then suddenly your output is not wordy enough?

Smells like OpenAI trying its best to stave off financial armageddon for another few months. Then again, I'm not sure why they chose to waste so much output computation on verbal diarrhea all this time up to now.

这部分令人困惑，因为它们并没有提供默认 GPT-5.6 输出与 GPT-5.5 的比较示例，两者均具有默认输出并提示简洁。 每当我使用此类提示时，通常是因为我希望模型用几句话告诉我要点。 如果 GPT-5.6 默认情况下如此简洁，我会感到震惊。 我认为对于那些不知道升级到 5.6 后立即进行更改的开发人员来说，这可能会“破坏”很多事情。 如果您期望 GPT 像平常一样冗长怎么办？ 然后突然间你的输出不够冗长？

children.author: tshaddox
children.textRawHtml:
If you conceptualize this as “there is an appropriate amount of brevity for each situation” then it would be expected for a better model to use different amounts of brevity if it gets better at determining the appropriate amount.

My view is that popular models by default output wildly excessive amounts of prose for nearly every use case, so if this changes in a new model that’s a pure win.
children.textZhHtml:
如果您将其概念化为“每种情况都有适当的简洁性”，那么如果更好的模型能够更好地确定适当的简洁性，那么预计会使用不同的简洁性。

我的观点是，流行的模型默认情况下会为几乎每个用例输出大量的散文，因此，如果在新模型中改变这种情况，那就是纯粹的胜利。

children.author: coldtea
children.textRawHtml:
>The models don't get better, except when a new one is released. Their performance depends solely on the model training before release and how well you curate the context you feed it. That's it.

Not quite. The hosting side can change reasoning budgets (or re-assign what terms like "high" means), temperature and other decoding parameters, output length limits, finetune internal "hidden" prompt, latency optimizations, finetune attention algorithms, even change quantization - all still serving as the same model.

We know (or suspect) Anthropic frequently nerfs models while keeping their name and version the same.
children.textZhHtml:
>除非发布新模型，否则模型不会变得更好。它们的性能完全取决于发布前的模型训练以及您对所提供的上下文的管理程度。就是这样。

不完全是。托管方可以更改推理预算（或重新分配“高”等术语的含义）、温度和其他解码参数、输出长度限制、微调内部“隐藏”提示、延迟优化、微调注意算法，甚至更改量化 - 所有这些仍然充当相同的模型。

我们知道（或怀疑）人为经常削弱模型，同时保持其名称和版本相同。

children.author: daveguy
children.textRawHtml: Right. They can do all those things. And none of that will make it smart or able to learn new things. The underlying model is just an llm. But judging from the downvotes, it seems AI folks get upset when someone talks honestly about their precious piles of matrix multiplication.
children.textZhHtml: 对。他们可以做所有这些事情。所有这些都不会使其变得聪明或能够学习新事物。底层模型只是一个 llm。但从反对票来看，当有人诚实地谈论他们宝贵的矩阵乘法时，人工智能人们似乎会感到不安。

children.author: Nevermark

Intelligence can operate without learning. At a minimum inference and learning don’t need to be co-concurrent.

children.author: sureMan6
children.textRawHtml: Might bother you to use anthropomorphic terminology like smart and learning but they are capable of producing work that traditionally required human intelligence and the whole point of gpt 3 was the ability to "learn", you can give it an example of an invented brand new coding language and it can write working code in that language
children.textZhHtml: 可能会打扰您使用智能和学习等拟人化术语，但它们能够产生传统上需要人类智能的工作，而 gpt 3 的全部要点就是“学习”的能力，您可以给它一个发明的全新编码语言的示例，它可以用该语言编写工作代码

children.author: kmacdough
children.textRawHtml:
Context is not the same as learning. It's easy to conflate because they're tightly coupled in our brains.

The underlying structure and tuning of the LLM are entirely unchanged by context. It merely affects the attention and activation of the network. The LLM will not be able to work with this hypothetical new language unless it is in context. This does not fit the computational meaning of learning.

Smart is not a well defined term. Nor is it's general idea formally understood. Use it freely, but you won't be saying anything meaningful unless you define your usage.
children.textZhHtml:
情境与学习不同。很容易混淆，因为它们在我们的大脑中紧密耦合。

LLM 的底层结构和调整完全不会因上下文而改变。它仅仅影响网络的注意力和激活。除非在上下文中，否则法学硕士将无法使用这种假设的新语言。这不符合学习的计算意义。

智能并不是一个定义明确的术语。它的总体思想也没有被正式理解。自由使用它，但除非您定义了用法，否则您不会说任何有意义的内容。

children.author: xylenox
children.textRawHtml: Yep, people always forget that early LLMs were sold as "Zero Shot Learning".
children.textZhHtml: 是的，人们总是忘记早期的法学硕士被称为“零次学习”。

children.author: kmacdough
children.textRawHtml:
Sold as learning, but that was a marketing term, not a technical one. From a technical perspective, the LLM is not learning. Only reacting based on its original training.

You might argue that the systems we've built around them are learning in a way, as they strategically condense and save artifacts from past interactions to pass into the LLMs context. But the LLM itself, which is the source of the intelligence, is not learning. It remains entirely unchanged throughout inference. This difference may seem trite, but it has significant impacts over the long term behavior.
children.textZhHtml:
作为学习内容出售，但这是一个营销术语，而不是技术术语。从技术角度来看，LLM并不是学习。仅根据其原始训练做出反应。

您可能会说，我们围绕它们构建的系统正在以某种方式学习，因为它们战略性地压缩并保存了过去交互中的工件，以传递到法学硕士环境中。但作为智力源泉的法学硕士本身并不是学习。在整个推理过程中它保持完全不变。这种差异可能看起来很老套，但它对长期行为具有重大影响。

children.author: coldtea
children.textRawHtml:
You used the word "smart" now, whereas on the comment I replied to, you said "better".

Tuning those can definitely make a model respond better or worse.

So your claim (quoting 100% as written) that "Their performance depends solely on the model training before release and how well you curate the context you feed it" is wrong. Hence the downvotes.

Doesn't matter if LLMs are to be considered intelligent or not for the claim to be wrong.

> But judging from the downvotes, it seems AI folks get upset when someone talks honestly about their precious piles of matrix multiplication.

Often yes. In this case, it's more like they get upset when someone says something factually wrong, and then defensively changes the goalposts.
children.textZhHtml:
你现在使用了“智能”这个词，而在我回复的评论中，你说的是“更好”。

调整这些绝对可以使模型响应更好或更差。

因此，你的主张（100%引用书面内容）“它们的性能完全取决于发布前的模型训练以及你如何管理你提供的上下文”是错误的。因此，投了反对票。

法学硕士是否被认为聪明并不重要，因为他们的说法是错误的。

>但从反对票来看，当有人诚实地谈论他们宝贵的矩阵乘法堆时，人工智能人员似乎会感到不安。

通常是的。在这种情况下，更像是当有人说一些事实上错误的事情时，他们会感到不安，然后防御性地改变球门柱。

children.author: daveguy
children.textRawHtml:
> Often yes. In this case, it's more like they get upset when someone says something factually wrong, and then defensively changes the goalposts.

Oh give me a break. Show me one example of 1) any knob twisting that makes the underlying model better. or 2) any example of the AI providers twisting those knobs to do anything other than degrade performance for their own bottom line or safety.

The current post says: "it would be expected for a better model to use different amounts of brevity if it gets better at determining the appropriate amount."

When no, the model cannot "get better". It doesn't determine any appropriateness of response realtime except for the weights baked into it from the beginning and whatever context it can muster. If you cram enough guidance that it doesn't decide to ignore maybe you can make it more brief. But it (the model) can do none of those things.

LLM models are literally stupid by design.
children.textZhHtml:
> 通常是的。在这种情况下，更像是当有人说了一些事实上错误的事情时，他们会感到不安，然后防御性地改变球门柱。

哦，让我休息一下。向我展示一个关于 1) 任何可以使底层模型变得更好的旋钮扭转的示例。或 2) 任何人工智能提供商为了自己的底线或安全而扭曲这些旋钮来做任何事情而不是降低性能的例子。

当前的帖子说：“如果更好的模型能够更好地确定适当的数量，那么预计更好的模型会使用不同数量的简洁性。”

如果不这样做，模型就无法“变得更好”。除了从一开始就融入其中的权重以及它可以聚集的任何上下文之外，它无法确定实时响应的任何适当性。如果你塞满了足够的指导，以至于它不会决定忽略，也许你可以让它更简短。但它（模型）不能做这些事情。

LLM 模型在设计上确实很愚蠢。

children.author: dahart
children.textRawHtml:
Why did you drop the first half of the sentence in your quote? The qualification there is important context for the part you did quote. And why are you talking about “better” within a model, when the sentence you quoted was talking about 5.6 vs 5.5? The post you’re referring to did not suggest a single model could “get better”. You’ve made some incorrect assumptions.

Your comments are conflating multiple kinds of “smart” and “better”. You’re right that if all the inputs are exactly the same, it takes a new model to improve (ignoring non-determinism). But the knobs and context and harness change the inputs, and they do improve output, contrary to your claim. You’re failing to capture the distinction between what the model itself does and how the harness can boost the model’s performance. It is legitimately valid and fair to call improved performance “better”, no matter where it comes from.

This all gives me the feeling you might not have experience with or understand what’s happening in today’s harness development, and the degree to which it may be as important as the weights. There are in fact a lot of things you can do to improve a model’s performance on tasks & benchmarks, without changing the model weights. @coldtea mentioned a bunch, but the harness feedback loop, internal prompts, system prompts, skills, and requests for a model to try harder, and verify and validate it’s output all lead to improved performance, all without retraining.

I agree LLMs are stupid; they’re statistical token predictors. But somehow statistical token prediction is amazing and works much better than we imagined. The talking points about LLMs being stupid token predictors are fading now because they lack explanatory power for how good the models have become. The big surprise here isn’t about LLMs. It’s about language, and how much “thinking” and intelligence is contained in language. We don’t have a good grasp on where the line is between language and intelligence. LLMs have crushed the Turing Test into dust, and yet we don’t consider them intelligent. They often appear to understand what you ask thoroughly, can re-state it in different words, they can correct your misunderstandings or add nuance you didn’t see. All this because that’s what humans do and LLMs talk like humans.
children.textZhHtml:
为什么您在引用中删除了前半句话？那里的资格对于您引用的部分有重要的背景。当您引用的句子谈论的是 5.6 与 5.5 时，为什么您要谈论模型中的“更好”？您所指的帖子并未暗示单个模型可以“变得更好”。您做出了一些错误的假设。

您的评论混淆了多种“聪明”和“更好”。你是对的，如果所有输入都完全相同，则需要一个新模型来改进（忽略非确定性）。但是旋钮、上下文和线束改变了输入，并且它们确实提高了输出，这与您的说法相反。您无法捕捉到模型本身的功能与线束如何提高模型性能之间的区别。称改进性能是合法有效且公平的“更好”，无论它来自哪里。

这一切都给我一种感觉，你可能没有经验或不了解当今安全带开发中发生的事情，以及它可能与配重一样重要的程度。事实上，您可以采取很多措施来提高模型在任务和基准测试中的性能，而无需更改模型权重。 @coldtea 提到了很多，但是利用反馈循环、内部提示、系统提示、技能以及对模型更加努力的请求以及验证和验证其输出都可以提高性能，而无需重新培训。

我同意法学硕士很愚蠢；它们是统计标记预测器。但不知何故，统计代币预测是惊人的，并且比我们想象的要好得多。关于法学硕士是愚蠢的代币预测者的话题现在正在逐渐消失，因为它们缺乏对模型变得有多好的解释力。这里最大的惊喜不是法学硕士。这是关于语言，以及语言中包含多少“思考”和智力。我们不太清楚语言和智力之间的界限在哪里。法学硕士已经将图灵测试粉碎成灰尘，但我们并不认为他们是聪明的。他们通常看起来完全理解你的问题，可以用不同的语言重新表述，他们可以纠正你的误解或添加你没有看到的细微差别。所有这一切都是因为这就是人类所做的事情，而法学硕士就像人类一样说话。

children.author: daveguy
children.textRawHtml:
> Why did you drop the first half of the sentence in your quote?

Because this entire discussion is about the release of a new model, and models are fixed. Sure you can try to modify all the scaffolding around it, but the model is the model. It doesn't matter what you're trying to improve. You can only improve the peripheral aides. And the peripheral aides can't fundamentally fix the problems with llm models when they can't learn new relationships or facts.

You will always have to wait for a new model (like this one we are talking about) for improvements to the model.
children.textZhHtml:
> 为什么你在引用中删除了前半句话？

因为整个讨论都是关于新模型的发布，而模型是固定的。当然你可以尝试修改它周围的所有脚手架，但模型就是模型。你想改进什么并不重要。你只能改进外围辅助。而外围助手如果无法学习新的关系或事实，就无法从根本上解决 llm 模型的问题。

你总是需要等待新模型（就像我们正在谈论的这个模型）来改进模型。

children.author: dahart
children.textRawHtml:
> Because this entire discussion is about the release of a new model

Right. The sentence you quoted was about brevity improving with a new model. It did not suggest the model itself improving.

I’m confused why you’re stuck on this tangent. And confused why you are repeating the talking points about the model being fixed. The model is fixed - that’s true, I already agreed with you. But you don’t seem to be listening to anything else.

> It doesn’t matter what you’re trying to improve.

What do you mean? If we’re trying to improve LLM output, there are multiple ways to achieve it. A new model is one of them. Changing the inputs is another.

> You will always have to wait for a new model (like this one we are talking about) for improvements to the model.

This is true! Nobody here is disagreeing with that. The part that it seems you’ve argued incorrectly is the apparent claim that output can’t get better. Output can “improve” without improving the model.
children.textZhHtml:
> 因为整个讨论都是关于新模型的发布

对。您引用的这句话是关于简洁使用新模型改进的。它并不表明模型本身有所改进。

我很困惑为什么你会卡在这个切线上。并困惑为什么你要重复有关修复模型的谈话要点。模型是固定的——是的，我已经同意你的观点了。但你似乎没有在听其他任何事情。

>你想改进什么并不重要。

你是什么意思？如果我们想提高 LLM 的产出，有多种方法可以实现。新模型就是其中之一。更改输入是另一回事。

>您将始终需要等待新模型（就像我们正在讨论的这个模型）才能改进模型。

这是真的！这里没有人不同意这一点。您认为错误的部分是明显声称产出无法变得更好。输出可以在不改进模型的情况下“改进”。

comments.author: fosk
comments.textRawHtml:
The EU is a farce, an undemocratic virtue signaling organization, and this is why:

- The Parliament voted against the first reading of this proposal twice in 2026, the first time they only supported limited cases for it, while the second time they actually defeated it fully.

- The Commission didn't care, and kept the proposal on the table by refusing to withdraw it.

- Once the Commission does that, the proposal goes on second-reading (despite the first-reading having defeated it) and it is established in a very PERVERSE way in EU law that to AVOID passing the proposal in second-reading you need ABSOLUTE majority which is incredibly hard to pursue (you would think that we would need an absolute majority to PASS a proposal that was previously defeated on first-reading, not instead needing absolute majority to DENY a previously defeated proposal that was again forced to the table).

- Furthermore, absences in practicality count as "No" on the rejection. So of course they scheduled the vote in the summer when notoriously there will be many absences.

By never withdrawing a defeated proposal they can effectively and in practicality pursue any agenda they want (it requires a massive mobilization effort to find absolute majority to defeat any proposal, especially when absences for any reason effectively count against rejection).

In PRACTICALITY, the Commission can pursue any agenda whenever and however they want, and throw the votes down the drain.

EU's democracy is lipstick on a pig.

children.author: rayiner

> he explained it correctly to his wife (American lawyer), or if she verified.

Are you saying I am mixing it up now? My post acknowledged I had mixed it up before: given that the Commission is so powerful, I had thought it was comprised of the heads of state. I’m aware that’s not how it’s structured. I was explaining it to my wife because the reality is very complicated!

> I don't like the way US presidents are elected either, ignoring popular vote and including gerrymandering.

Gerrymandering cannot affect a Presidential election, because the Electoral College is elected based on statewide popular votes. And the Electoral College is somewhere in-between direct election of the chief executive, as in France, and indirect election of the chief executive, as in most european countries.

In every case where the U.S. electoral college vote produced a different result than the national popular vote, the President’s party still won the Congressional popular vote. So the outcome would have been the same if our rules were like most of Europe, where the winning party picks the chief executive.
children.textZhHtml:
> 他向他的妻子（美国律师）正确解释了这一点，或者她是否证实了。

你是说我现在混淆了吗？我的帖子承认我之前把它搞混了：鉴于委员会如此强大，我以为它是由国家元首组成的。我知道它的结构不是这样的。我正在向我的妻子解释，因为现实很复杂！

>我也不喜欢美国总统的选举方式，无视普选，还包括不公正地划分选区。

不公正地划分选区不会影响总统选举，因为选举团是根据全州普选票选出的。选举团制度介于行政长官直接选举（如法国）和行政长官间接选举（如大多数欧洲国家）之间。

在美国选举团投票产生与全国普选结果不同的情况下，总统所在的政党仍然赢得了国会普选。因此，如果我们的规则像欧洲大多数国家一样，由获胜政党选出首席执行官，结果也会是一样的。

He literally ignores a lot more complexities and drama surrounding a lot of issues. Does he ramble sometimes? Maybe, but the talks so fast at times that the video is over before I've even gotten half way through with my own research. children.author: Zancarius

"An account of human suffering" (assuming I remember the approximate title) is one of his earlier videos that sticks out in my mind and one I'll never forget. It was a tongue-in-cheek nod to the fact board diagnostics for hardware that has obviously been abused by the end user is a tedious and often thankless process.

I learned a LOT from watching his videos, and I don't even do board repair! Psychology, business, the human condition...

我从观看他的视频中学到了很多东西，而且我什至不进行主板维修！心理学、商业、人类状况……

children.author: Shocka1
children.textRawHtml:
Almost every conversation I've had over the years goes two ways: "Why do you care so much about <Flock, Ring Cameras, big tech name>? If you have nothing to hide, why are you so concerned?"

Or:

"Why do you care so much about <Flock, Ring Cameras, big tech name>? We already walk around with cell phones in our pockets 24/7."

I used to be more passionate about persuading people about privacy, but I've pretty much given up. I've come to the conclusion that the majority of the population doesn't care what is happening around them as long as they have internet access and their iPhone.
children.textZhHtml:
多年来，我经历的几乎每一次对话都分为两种情况： “你为什么这么在意<Flock、Ring摄像头、大型科技公司名称>？如果你没做什么见不得人的事，干嘛这么担心？”

或者：

“你为什么这么在意<Flock、Ring摄像头、大型科技公司名称>？反正我们现在都是24小时手机不离身。”

过去我曾更热衷于劝说人们关注隐私问题，但现在我基本放弃了。我得出的结论是，只要还能上网、还能用iPhone，大多数人根本不在乎周围正在发生什么。

children.author: archontes
children.textRawHtml:
My response is typically, "I have literally everything to hide. I don't want you to know my middle name. I don't want you to know what I had for breakfast. It's none of anyone's business."

And weirdly, the response I get is, "Oh yeah, fair."
children.textZhHtml:
我通常的回答是：“我简直有无数事情要隐瞒。我不想让你知道我的中间名，也不想让你知道我早餐吃了什么。这跟任何人都没关系。”

而奇怪的是，我得到的回应却是：“哦，也是，说得在理。”

stories.title: Postgres rewritten in Rust, now passing 100% of the Postgres regression tests
stories.titleZh: 使用 Rust 重写的 Postgres，现已 100% 通过 Postgres 回归测试

comments.author: malisper
comments.textRawHtml:
Hey author here. Wasn't expecting to see this up.

To concisely give an overview of the project, I've been experimenting with using LLMs to build a better version of Postgres. Postgres is 30 years old and we've learned a lot about databases since hten. A lot of the techniques that work for doing a rewrite are also useful for doing a rearchitecture.

I'm now working on a new, not yet published version of pgrust that incorporates a lot of techniques. Currently the new version:

  - Passes 100% of Postgres regression suite
  - Implements a thread per connection model instead of the process per connection model Postgres does
  - Is 50% faster than Postgres on transaction workloads
  - Is ~300x faster than Postgres on analytical workloads. Right now it's 2x slower than Clickhouse on clickbench and I think it's possible to get faster than Clickhouse
If you have any questions, I'm happy to answer them.
comments.textZhHtml:
嘿，作者。没想到会看到这个。

为了简洁地概述该项目，我一直在尝试使用 LLM 来构建更好的 Postgres 版本。 Postgres 已经有 30 岁了，从那时起我们就学到了很多关于数据库的知识。许多用于重写的技术对于重新架构也很有用。

我现在正在开发一个新的、尚未发布的 prust 版本，它包含了很多技术。目前新版本：

 - 通过 100% Postgres 回归套件
  - 实现每个连接一个线程模型，而不是 Postgres 的每个连接进程模型
  - 在事务工作负载上比 Postgres 快 50%
  - 在分析工作负载上比 Postgres 快大约 300 倍。目前，它在 clickbench 上比 Clickhouse 慢 2 倍，我认为有可能比 Clickhouse 更快
如果您有任何问题，我很乐意回答。

children.author: Keyframe
children.textRawHtml:
I don't want to knock you down as most have already did. In-fact it's a useful exercise going forward in exploring how to work with AI. It's here, we're all going to use it one way or the other. Zero issues with that, in-fact kudos to going through the pain of it all.

Now, having gone through several such endeavors originally myself, albeit with internal tools and systems (as an exercise), I've noticed that while all my tests passed with flying colors the rewrite itself was broken even on basic functionality or missed a ton of details. It was in effect useless when I dived into it. Initial tests also showed massive gain in performance, and I know people who were involved aren't really dumb so something smelled funny. Turns out all those things left out and honestly... moments were the key ingredients.

What I did learn from those beginning explorations though was that one-shotting, grand architecture or source up-front, master plans up-front.. all these do not yield good results - YET. Who know what we'll see in few years though. What I did found that works (FOR ME, nota bene) is to keep the design and checklists for myself, written by myself and then do a small piece by piece.. as if you would if you were coding alone or if you would waterfalling a small team of talented juniors. Then, suddenly super happy results come out, but then it's mostly you driving all the way where llm writes code and offers advice (which for the most part you ignore). It's a happy place for myself at least. It's then truly unlocking yourself to the mythical 10x.

Rewriting a large proven system with decades of ultra expertise behind it, which I don't have, is guaranteed not to end up the same 1:1 replacement. If you found a recipe for that - please do share.
children.textZhHtml:
我不想像大多数人那样把你击倒。事实上，这是探索如何使用人工智能的一个有用的练习。它就在这里，我们都会以一种或另一种方式使用它。零问题，事实上，值得庆幸的是经历了这一切的痛苦。

现在，我自己经历了几次这样的努力，尽管使用了内部工具和系统（作为练习），但我注意到，虽然我的所有测试都顺利通过，但重写本身甚至在基本功能上也被破坏了，或者错过了大量细节。当我深入研究它时，它实际上毫无用处。最初的测试也显示出性能的巨大提升，而且我知道参与其中的人并不傻，所以有些东西闻起来很有趣。事实证明，所有这些被遗漏的东西和老实说......时刻是关键要素。

不过，我从那些开始的探索中确实学到的是一次性的、宏伟的架构或预先的源代码、预先的总体规划..所有这些都不会产生好的结果 - 但。谁知道几年后我们会看到什么。我发现工作（对我来说，nota bene）是为我自己保留设计和清单，由我自己编写，然后一点一点地做......就像你独自编码或如果你要组建一个由才华横溢的初级团队组成的小团队一样。然后，突然超级高兴的结果出来了，但随后主要是你一路开车，llm编写代码并提供建议（大多数情况下你忽略了）。至少对我自己来说这是一个快乐的地方。然后，它真正将您自己解锁到神话般的 10 倍。

重写一个经过验证的大型系统，并在其背后拥有数十年的超级专业知识（而我没有），保证不会最终导致相同的 1:1 替换。如果您找到了一个食谱 - 请分享。

children.author: film42
children.textRawHtml: A thread per connection is a almost always the correct decision for performance, but by choosing a process per connection, postgres is able to let you load whatever sketchy extensions you want. Worst case you crash the process, not the database. It would be nice if you could strike a balance so a segfaul in the extension only crashes a small percentage of connections, not the whole thing.
children.textZhHtml: 每个连接一个线程对于性能来说几乎总是正确的决定，但是通过为每个连接选择一个进程，postgres 能够让您加载您想要的任何粗略扩展。最坏的情况是进程崩溃，而不是数据库崩溃。如果您能够取得平衡，这样扩展中的段错误只会导致一小部分连接崩溃，而不是整个连接崩溃，那就太好了。

children.author: pilif
children.textRawHtml:
That’s not true for Postgres however: due to its usage of a shared memory pool, whenever a subprocess is terminated unexpectedly, Postgres will kill all other processes and enter recovery mode, replaying the WAL, during which time it will not accept connection requests.

It does this because it can’t possibly know whether the dying process did bad things to the shared memory pool.
children.textZhHtml:
但是对于 Postgres 来说情况并非如此：由于它使用共享内存池，每当子进程意外终止时，Postgres 都会杀死所有其他进程并进入恢复模式，重播 WAL，在此期间它不会接受连接请求。

它这样做是因为它不可能知道垂死的进程是否对共享内存池做了坏事。

children.author: film42
children.textRawHtml: You are correct! TIL, and thank you. Connection processes get a SIGQUIT, shared buffers cleared, and WAL replayed, but postmaster stays alive. It's effectively an online restart.
children.textZhHtml: 你是对的！直到，谢谢你。连接进程收到 SIGQUIT、共享缓冲区被清除、WAL 被重放，但 postmaster 仍保持活动状态。这实际上是一次在线重启。

children.author: anp
children.textRawHtml: I’d be very curious to hear what an online restart approach for sketchy extensions crashing a shared thread per connection process might look like. This is more a question for the author of the project but I’m curious if they have plans in that direction.
children.textZhHtml: 我很好奇，对于导致每个连接进程的共享线程崩溃的粗略扩展的在线重启方法可能是什么样子。这更多是项目作者的问题，但我很好奇他们是否有朝这个方向的计划。

children.author: funny_falcon
children.textRawHtml:
systems, upstart or even simple primitive script wrapper.

Built-in is also possible: just fork once after start, and you have parent as watchdog and restarter.
children.textZhHtml:
系统、暴发户甚至简单的原始脚本包装器。

内置也是可能的：启动后只需分叉一次，并且您有父级作为看门狗和重新启动器。

children.author: vanviegen
children.textRawHtml: What's online about this restart? Just that the tcp port keeps listening? I assume all running transaction will have to be aborted, right? And connections dropped?
children.textZhHtml: 网上关于这次重启的消息有哪些？只是tcp端口一直监听？我认为所有正在运行的事务都必须中止，对吧？连接断开了？

children.author: film42
children.textRawHtml: The postmaster is effectively your supervisor. It could see the child segfault and abort the whole DB, but that would be deferring supervision to your init system. Not ideal.
children.textZhHtml: 邮政局长实际上是您的主管。它可以看到子段错误并中止整个数据库，但这将推迟对 init 系统的监督。不理想。

children.author: fpgaminer
children.textRawHtml: If it's a choice between performance and being able to "safely" run sketchy extensions, I'd rather have performance.
children.textZhHtml: 如果要在性能和能够“安全”运行粗略扩展之间进行选择，我宁愿选择性能。

children.author: brianaker
children.textRawHtml:
A mixture of threads and processes that can be used to match processors, disk I/O, and network interfaces.

A very long time ago, there was once a feature called "Data Blades" which tanked a commercial database vendor. A badly behaving blade could bring down the entire database. Most anyone who has been working on databases for a few decades remembers this and makes a point of either not introducing these sorts of features or making use of processes over threads.

I have not looked at the code referenced in the mentioned project, but thus far I haven't seen a model that could craft a complete SQL parser on its own.

There are a number of problems, and design decisions, that a developer decides on when writing a database that I don't see any current models… just because you have the ingredients does not mean that the stew is edible.
children.textZhHtml:
线程和进程的混合体，可用于匹配处理器、磁盘 I/O 和网络接口。

很久以前，曾经有一个名为“数据刀片”的功能让一家商业数据库供应商陷入困境。表现不佳的刀片可能会导致整个数据库瘫痪。大多数从事数据库工作几十年的人都记得这一点，并强调要么不引入此类功能，要么在线程上使用进程。

我没有查看上述项目中引用的代码，但到目前为止，我还没有看到一个可以自行构建完整 SQL 解析器的模型。

开发人员在编写我没有看到的数据库时会决定许多问题和设计决策。当前型号......仅仅因为您有成分并不意味着炖菜可以食用。

children.author: quantified
children.textRawHtml: Informix. Michael Stonebraker, to his credit, learned a lot along the way and revised his thinking about database technology and capability after believing that a single engine could be good at everything.
children.textZhHtml: Informix。值得赞扬的是，Michael Stonebraker 一路走来学到了很多东西，并在相信单一引擎可以胜任一切之后，修正了他对数据库技术和功能的看法。

children.author: mtlynch
children.textRawHtml:
Yes, "their" refers to Bun's code, not the Zig compiler's code. Fuzzili is a fuzzing engine for JavaScript, so integrating it into Bun means that Fuzzili is fuzzing Bun.[0]

From the Bun post[1]

> We fuzz Bun's runtime APIs 24/7 using Fuzzilli, the JavaScript engine fuzzer used by V8 & JavaScriptCore

From Andrew Kelley's post today[2]:

> The post claims they were fuzzing their Zig code, while during our calls the whole Bun team told us that they were not fuzzing anything. This appears to be an outright fabrication.

Sumner says that the Bun team has been fuzzing Bun's Zig code. Kelley says that this is a fabrication. Sumner showed proof that the Bun team has been fuzzing Bun's Zig code.

It looks like Kelley is incorrect and made an unfounded claim. The generous interpretation is that at the time Kelley and Sumner had a more collaborative relationship, Sumner was not fuzzing Bun's Zig code, but I'd expect Kelley to check if anything had changed since then before publicly accusing Sumner of lying in this week's Bun blog post.

[0] https://github.com/googleprojectzero/fuzzilli

[1] https://bun.com/blog/bun-in-rust

[2] https://andrewkelley.me/post/my-thoughts-bun-rust-rewrite.ht...
children.textZhHtml:
是的，“他们的”指的是 Bun 的代码，而不是 Zig 编译器的代码。 Fuzzili 是 JavaScript 的模糊测试引擎，因此将其集成到 Bun 意味着 Fuzzili 正在对 Bun 进行模糊测试。[0]

来自 Bun 帖子[1]

> 我们使用 Fuzzilli（V8 和 JavaScriptCore 使用的 JavaScript 引擎模糊器）对 Bun 的运行时 API 进行 24/7 模糊测试

来自 Andrew Kelley 今天的帖子[2]：

> 该帖子声称他们正在模糊测试他们的 Zig 代码，而在我们的通话中，整个 Bun 团队告诉我们他们没有模糊测试任何东西。这似乎是彻头彻尾的捏造。

Sumner 表示，Bun 团队一直在模糊 Bun 的 Zig 代码。凯利说这是捏造的。 Sumner 展示了 Bun 团队一直在模糊 Bun 的 Zig 代码的证据。

看起来 Kelley 是错误的，并且提出了毫无根据的主张。慷慨的解释是，当时凯利和萨姆纳有更多的合作关系，萨姆纳没有对 Bun 的 Zig 代码进行模糊测试，但我希望凯利在公开指责萨姆纳在本周的 Bun 博客文章中撒谎之前检查自那以后是否有任何变化。

[0] https://github.com/googleprojectzero/fuzzilli

[1] https://bun.com/blog/bun-in-rust

[2] https://andrewkelley.me/post/my-thoughts-bun-rust-rewrite.ht...

children.author: xlii
children.textRawHtml:
AFAIU fuzzing code != fuzzing results. Through skimming it seems that integration tests were using fuzzing, but I would call it fuzzing the code itself.

From "product" perspective there's no difference, but in program-compiler perspective (and e.g. raising bugs about compiler), Fuzilli isn't fuzzing.

Per Wikipedia > (then...) The program is then monitored for exceptions such as crashes, failing built-in code assertions, or potential memory leaks.

As for myself, I wouldn't use term fuzzing for integration testing such the one used by Fuzilla. I always caught it dynamic testing, scenario testing and in bigger cases property based tests. Fuzzing in my mind is reserved to a low-abstraction calls.

Might just be me, though.
children.textZhHtml:
AFAIU 模糊测试代码！= 模糊测试结果。通过略读，集成测试似乎使用了模糊测试，但我将其称为对代码本身进行模糊测试。

从“产品”的角度来看，没有区别，但从程序编译器的角度来看（例如，引发有关编译器的错误），Fuzilli 并不是模糊测试。

根据维基百科 >（然后...）然后监视程序是否存在异常，例如崩溃、内置代码断言失败或潜在的内存泄漏。

就我自己而言，我不会使用术语模糊测试来进行集成测试，例如 Fuzilla 使用的术语。我总是抓住它动态测试、场景测试以及在更大的情况下基于属性的测试。我脑海中的模糊测试仅限于低抽象调用。

不过，可能只是我。

children.author: jsnell
children.textRawHtml:
I don't understand what distinction you're trying to draw here. The very specific claim[0] in the Bun blog post that Kelley is calling a fabrication was:

> We fuzz Bun's runtime APIs 24/7 using Fuzzilli, the JavaScript engine fuzzer used by V8 & JavaScriptCore

It does not look to be a fabrication, and is very explicit just about what they meant by fuzzing.

[0] I mean, that sentence doesn't actually match Kelley's paraphrase, but it is literally the only claim in the post related to what fuzzing was done on the Zig-based bun codebase. So it has to be what Kelley was referring to, and his paraphrase is as sloppy as his fact-checking.
children.textZhHtml:
我不明白你想在这里做出什么区别。 Bun 博客文章中 Kelley 称之为捏造的非常具体的声明[0]是：

> 我们使用 Fuzzilli（V8 和 JavaScriptCore 使用的 JavaScript 引擎模糊器）对 Bun 的运行时 API 24/7 进行模糊测试

它看起来不是捏造的，并且非常明确地说明了模糊测试的含义。

[0] 我的意思是，这句话实际上与 Kelley 的释义不符，但它是从字面上看，该帖子中唯一的声明与基于 Zig 的 Bun 代码库上进行的模糊测试有关。所以这一定是凯利所指的，而他的释义和他的事实核查一样草率。

children.author: xlii
children.textRawHtml:
For me, using Fuzzilli for testing a Zig code is not fuzzing, it's integration testing. If you're running code externally (e.g. wrapping binary) you cannot guarantee that side effect isn't caused by IO. I consider fuzzing a low level activity with many external variables removed.

Depending on where you are and how you communicate semantics matter more or less. It's very similar to compiler/transpiler. E.g. TypeScript "Compiler" is called compiler but in fact it's transpiler (it emits other high-level language as a result).
children.textZhHtml:
对我来说，使用 Fuzzilli 测试 Zig 代码不是模糊测试，而是集成测试。如果您在外部运行代码（例如包装二进制文件），则无法保证副作用不是由 IO 引起的。我考虑对低级活动进行模糊测试，并删除许多外部变量。

取决于您所在的位置以及您如何传达语义，这或多或少很重要。它与编译器/转译器非常相似。例如。 TypeScript“编译器”被称为编译器，但实际上它是转译器（结果是生成其他高级语言）。

children.author: jsnell
children.textRawHtml:
My point is that Kelley did not argue that what Bun does isn't really fuzzing. He wrote that the post's claim is a fabrication. But that claim is really specific, and to evaluate whether it is true it doesn't matter what Kelley's unstated definition of fuzzing is.

So an argument about definitions doesn't seem super valuable here.
children.textZhHtml:
我的观点是，Kelley 并没有争论 Bun 所做的并不是真正的模糊测试。他写道，该帖子的说法纯属捏造。但这种说法确实很具体，要评估它是否属实，凯利未阐明的模糊测试定义是什么并不重要。

因此，关于定义的争论在这里似乎没有多大价值。

children.author: mtlynch
children.textRawHtml:
> For me, using Fuzzilli for testing a Zig code is not fuzzing, it's integration testing. If you're running code externally (e.g. wrapping binary) you cannot guarantee that side effect isn't caused by IO. I consider fuzzing a low level activity with many external variables removed.

I've never heard anyone restrict the definition of "fuzzing" in this way. If I repeatedly generate inputs to a program and then run the program with those inputs, that's fuzzing. It doesn't matter if there's IO or not.

> Depending on where you are and how you communicate semantics matter more or less. It's very similar to compiler/transpiler. E.g. TypeScript "Compiler" is called compiler but in fact it's transpiler (it emits other high-level language as a result).

It's still a compiler. It translates code from one language to another. You can argue whether we need the term "transpiler," but a source-to-source compiler is a compiler.
children.textZhHtml:
> 对我来说，使用 Fuzzilli 测试 Zig 代码不是模糊测试，而是集成测试。如果您在外部运行代码（例如包装二进制文件），则无法保证副作用不是由 IO 引起的。我认为模糊测试是一项低级活动，删除了许多外部变量。

我从未听说过有人以这种方式限制“模糊测试”的定义。如果我重复生成程序的输入，然后使用这些输入运行程序，那就是模糊测试。有没有 IO 并不重要。

> 或多或少取决于你在哪里以及你如何传达语义。它与编译器/转译器非常相似。例如。 TypeScript“编译器”被称为编译器，但实际上它是转译器（因此它会生成其他高级语言）。

它仍然是一个编译器。它将代码从一种语言翻译成另一种语言。您可以争论我们是否需要术语“转译器”，但源到源编译器就是编译器。

children.author: ffsm8
children.textRawHtml:
> You can argue whether we need the term "transpiler," but a source-to-source compiler is a compiler.

That's true today, but compiling was historically was defined as getting source code (human readable) to bytecode (machine runnable without an interpreter).

Some people didn't like that definition, and consequently the waters have been murkied. Just like with eg crypto. Or real time.
children.textZhHtml:
> 你可能会争论我们是否需要“转译器”这个术语，但源到源的编译器就是一个编译器。

今天确实如此，但编译在历史上被定义为将源代码（人类可读）转换为字节码（无需解释器即可机器运行）。

有些人不喜欢这个定义，因此水域变得浑浊。就像加密货币一样。或实时。

comments.author: didibus
comments.textRawHtml:
Article did a decent job of showing discipline and care and human involvement to assert the automated rewrite was done diligently, as best as it can be when using AI for it. I does make me feel a bit more comfortable about it.

As an aside, I don't know why anyone would not want to use a memory-safe (and possibly race-safe) language in 2026. Rust gives you that in a performant package, so if you are turned off by GCs and immutability for performance reasons, you still have the option to use Rust.

I can understand when you need the absolute best performance and you decide to drop to down to C++, and I also relate with just personal preference, but beyond those it seems a no brainer to me.
comments.textZhHtml:
文章很好地展示了纪律、关怀和人为参与，以断言自动重写是勤奋完成的，在使用人工智能时已尽最大努力。我确实让我对此感觉更舒服。

顺便说一句，我不知道为什么有人不想在 2026 年使用内存安全（可能还有种族安全）的语言。Rust 在一个高性能包中为您提供了这一点，因此，如果您出于性能原因被 GC 和不变性关闭，您仍然可以选择使用 Rust。

我可以理解何时您需要绝对最佳的性能，并且您决定降级到 C++，我也仅与个人喜好相关，但除此之外，这对我来说似乎是理所当然的。

children.author: leecommamichael
children.textRawHtml:
> As an aside, I don't know why anyone would not want to use a memory-safe (and possibly race-safe) language in 2026.

The rust compiler is very slow. The best way to speed it up appears to be organizing a codebase in many crates. This is not preferable ergonomics to many. Beside that, for many problems, a garbage collector eliminates a large amount of defects (including the ones stated in the article) without any added friction, whereas Rust asks that you think in terms of ownership. This is not preferable ergonomics to many.

I realize what I'm saying above, while true, doesn't give a clear example. Many gamedevs would rather iterate with a language that is lower friction, not only because game code is finnicky (like frontend UI code) but because the build process can be unique. Many gamedevs prefer to iterate with hot-reloading, and asking them to use a slower compiler is asking them to accept greater latency in that cycle.

I do not claim that these reasons apply to everyone.
children.textZhHtml:
> 顺便说一句，我不知道为什么有人不想在 2026 年使用内存安全（可能还有竞争安全）的语言。

Rust 编译器非常慢。加快速度的最佳方法似乎是在许多箱子中组织代码库。对于许多人来说，这并不是优选的人体工程学。除此之外，对于许多问题，垃圾收集器可以消除大量缺陷（包括文章中所述的缺陷），而不会增加任何摩擦，而 Rust 要求您从所有权角度进行思考。对于许多人来说，这并不是更好的人体工程学。

我意识到我上面所说的虽然正确，但没有给出一个明确的例子。许多游戏开发人员宁愿使用摩擦较低的语言进行迭代，不仅因为游戏代码很繁琐（如前端 UI 代码），而且因为构建过程可能是唯一的。许多游戏开发者更喜欢通过热重载进行迭代，要求他们使用较慢的编译器就等于要求他们接受该周期中更大的延迟。

我并不认为这些原因适用于所有人。

children.author: zamalek
children.textRawHtml:
Game engines are typically in two languages, one for the engine itself and one for scripting. That even goes for Unity: in Unity, C# is a significantly more powerful than average scripting language (for lack of a better term), but the engine itself is still C++.

That's not to say that you couldn't write a commercial game engine with something like C# that stands shoulder-to-shoulder with unity and unreal, but it doesn't seem like anyone has attempted to do so. Maybe it's the decompilation fear.

Also, it would continue to make sense to use a scripting language alongside Rust.

children.author: ACCount37
children.textRawHtml:
Mostly, because a lot of game engines are ancient relics, tracing their lineage all the way back to Quake 1.

The development practices are not exactly up to date, and game development is in no hurry to change. It doesn't help that software development wages there are not at all competitive - game development selects for passion, not skill. People who want to build robust modern codebases and people who want to build AAA games are different people. So there aren't many game devs who want to push for better test coverage.

But it's also because game engines are dealing with many, many things that are hard to test for.

You know how messy it is to test a website for "does this layout look right" or "can you navigate from A to B"? Now multiply that by complex 3D geometry. A lot of what game engines do is dealing with complex 3D geometry, where the primary verification is "does it look right" and "does this interaction feel right". Which is why game development traditionally has wide human QA, and slim unit testing.

Only now do we have software that can sort of, semi-reliably, automate testing for "does it look right".
children.textZhHtml:
主要是因为很多游戏引擎都是古老的遗迹，其血统可以追溯到《Quake 1》。

开发实践并不完全是最新的，游戏开发并不急于改变。那里的软件开发工资根本没有竞争力，这也无济于事——游戏开发选择的是激情，而不是技能。想要构建强大的现代代码库的人和想要构建 AAA 游戏的人是不​​同的人。因此，没有多少游戏开发者想要推动更好的测试覆盖率。

但这也是因为游戏引擎正在处理很多很多难以测试的事情。

你知道测试一个网站“这个布局看起来正确吗”或“你能从 A 导航到 B”是多么混乱吗？现在将其乘以复杂的 3D 几何形状。游戏引擎所做的很多事情都是处理复杂的 3D 几何图形，其中主要验证是“它看起来正确吗”和“这种交互感觉正确吗”。这就是为什么游戏开发传统上需要广泛的人工 QA 和精简的单元测试。

直到现在，我们才有了可以半可靠地自动测试“它看起来正确吗”的软件。

children.author: mike_hearn
children.textRawHtml:
A game engine has a vast space of possible outputs, all of which will be considered good enough.

Its primary output is pixels and sound. Those are hard to test in a reasonable way. Screenshot testing is useless in a codebase where most of the changes are about making the pixels prettier.
children.textZhHtml:
游戏引擎有大量可能的输出，所有这些都被认为足够好。

它的主要输出是像素和声音。这些很难以合理的方式进行测试。屏幕截图测试在大多数更改都是为了使像素更漂亮的代码库中毫无用处。

children.author: hvb2
children.textRawHtml:
> In general I'm not a big fan of unit test on simple websites, they do not give any real value compared to effort

And then your site gets more traction, features are added, things are refactored.

And now you're looking at what used to be a simple site, still with no tests.

It's a habit, that pays off long term. You don't do it for yourself, you do it for who changes the code next. Might be you, or not. Even when you do it yourself, you can't hold all the requirements in you brain, you write those up in tests

children.author: zamalek
children.textRawHtml:
One option would be to have an input replay alongside captured outputs (audio visual), at some fixed framerate. Capturing intermediates (scene graph etc.) would probably also be valuable, as that could help nail down why something is failing.

Or you could do it [as I recall the project being called] the scientist way. You still have the old code, so you could replay inputs against each and compare. Probably more realistic because uncompressed video would be a ridiculously huge dataset. This would be more resilient in the face of testing hardware and driver drift.

Historically game engines are the worst offenders when it comes to unit testing. I'm not sure if that's still the case - but that's why I erred on the side of integration tests.
children.textZhHtml:
一种选择是以某个固定帧速率与捕获的输出（音频视频）一起重放输入。捕获中间体（场景图等）可能也很有价值，因为这可以帮助确定某些事情失败的原因。

或者您可以以科学家的方式做到这一点（我记得该项目被称为）。您仍然拥有旧代码，因此您可以针对每个代码重放输入并进行比较。可能更现实，因为未压缩的视频将是一个非常大的数据集。这在面对测试硬件和驱动程序漂移时会更具弹性。

从历史上看，游戏引擎在单元测试方面是最糟糕的。我不确定情况是否仍然如此 - 但这就是为什么我在集成测试方面犯了错误。

children.author: stymaar
children.textRawHtml:
> The rust compiler is very slow.

It's not “very slow”, that's a tired meme. It's slower than it could/should, but complaining about rustc being “very slow” is a clear misrepresentation, especially when everybody seems to have been fine with tsc's historical performance for instance. It could be nice if it was faster indeed, but people claiming it's “very slow” are just showing they never worked with it.

> The best way to speed it up appears to be organizing a codebase in many crates. This is not preferable ergonomics to many.

In this context (where you don't plan on publishing you stuff on crates.io) a “crate” are just a directory at the root of your repo, the ergonomic impact is literally zero.
children.textZhHtml:
> rust 编译器非常慢。

这不是“非常慢”，这是一个令人厌倦的模因。它比它可以/应该的要慢，但是抱怨 rustc“非常慢”显然是一种误解，尤其是当每个人似乎都对 tsc 的历史表现感到满意时。如果它确实更快的话那就太好了，但声称它“非常慢”的人只是表明他们从未使用过它。

> 加快速度的最佳方法似乎是在许多箱子中组织代码库。对于许多人来说，这不是更好的人体工程学。

在这种情况下（您不打算在 crates.io 上发布您的东西），“crate”只是存储库根目录中的一个目录，人体工程学影响实际上为零。

The average cargo check for the projects I've worked on, usually finish in less than 1 second, with `cargo build` completing in a single digit second (often below 2s), it's not slow by any means.

> I believe c, Fortran, zig, C#, Java and golang are all faster compiling languages.

Sure, but the difference between type checking is 10ms and type checking in 500ms is barely noticeable for a human being anyway, despite the x50 difference.

> That makes rust pretty slow in my book.

“Slow” is a perceptual thing. It doesn't matter if it's slower in absolute benchmark performance. If it doesn't slow you down in your work it's not “slow”.

> As far as I know the only language that compiles slower is C++

Typescript's compiler is much slower than Rust's, but it's plenty fast enough for most people and you almost never see complains about it because it mostly doesn't matter outside of pissing contests.
children.textZhHtml:
> 为什么你认为它不慢？

我做过的项目的平均货物检查通常在不到 1 秒内完成，其中 `cargo build` 在个位数秒内完成（通常低于 2 秒），无论如何都不慢。

> 我相信 c、Fortran、zig、C#、Java 和 golang 编译速度都更快

当然，但是类型检查之间的差异是 10 毫秒，而类型检查 500 毫秒对于人类来说几乎察觉不到，尽管有 50 倍的差异。

> 在我的书中，这使得 rust 相当慢。

“慢”是一个感性的东西。绝对基准性能是否较慢并不重要。如果它不会减慢你的工作速度，那么它就不是“慢”。

>据我所知，唯一编译速度较慢的语言是 C++

Typescript 的编译器比 Rust 慢得多，但对于大多数人来说已经足够快了，你几乎从未看到过对此的抱怨，因为除了小便比赛之外，它基本上无关紧要。

children.author: iainmerrick
children.textRawHtml:
Typescript's compiler is much slower than Rust's, but it's plenty fast enough for most people and you almost never see complains about it because it mostly doesn't matter

But you have to compile Rust code to run it. You can run TypeScript code without type-checking it. That’s a massive difference in the development workflow.

The new TSC, supposedly 10x faster, will be very pleasant to have but not as much of a game-changer as you might expect. A 10x faster Rust compiler would be incredible.
children.textZhHtml:
Typescript 的编译器比 Rust 慢得多，但对于大多数人来说已经足够快了，而且你几乎不会看到对此的抱怨，因为它基本上并不重要

但是你必须编译 Rust 代码来运行它。您可以运行 TypeScript 代码而无需进行类型检查。这是开发工作流程中的巨大差异。

新的 TSC 据称速度提高了 10 倍，拥有起来会非常令人愉快，但并没有您想象的那样改变游戏规则。如果 Rust 编译器速度提高 10 倍，那就太不可思议了。

children.author: jldugger
children.textRawHtml:
I think the request here is not about sounding like Majel Barrett but in keeping the output extremely terse and unobtrusive.

There's been a few studys showing that novices love LLM output that's long, but experts hate it. As an example, I've been tasked with using some agentic PM tool to write specs, and it keeps generating these huge page long outputs with "HBR voice" bolded summaries of paragraph long bulletpoints. I.e.:

> Right-size hard, and watch the one open-ended edge. Endorse the DRI's simplifications wholesale: drop the runbook-per-alert mandate (keep 1–2 diagnostic-only runbooks for the high-priority set), and ride durability on the existing weekly incident + monthly operational reviews — no new governance. The single scope-creep risk is the coverage strand (gaps are defined by absence); bound it to gaps evidenced by real, already-missed customer-facing outages, not a proactive gap hunt. Curing ownership gaps (e.g. foo-bar, no clear owner) is finite in-scope work.

There's dozens of these every iteration. I can't imagine trying to deal with that via voice, I would just zone out after the second sentence.
children.textZhHtml:
我认为这里的要求不是听起来像 Majel Barrett，而是要保持输出极其简洁和不引人注目。

有一些研究表明，新手喜欢 LLM 输出很长，但专家讨厌它。举个例子，我的任务是使用一些代理 PM 工具来编写规范，它不断生成这些大页长的输出，并带有“HBR 语音”段落长项目符号的粗体摘要。即：

> 适当大小的硬性，并观察一个开放式边缘。大规模认可 DRI 的简化：放弃每个警报的运行手册要求（为高优先级集保留 1-2 个仅用于诊断的运行手册），并在现有的每周事件 + 每月运营审查上实现持久性 - 无需新的治理。单一范围蔓延风险是覆盖链（间隙由缺席定义）；将其与实际的、已经错过的面向客户的中断所证明的差距联系起来，而不是主动寻找差距。消除所有权差距（例如 foo-bar，没有明确的所有者）是有限的范围内工作。

每次迭代都有数十个这样的工作。我无法想象尝试通过语音来处理这个问题，我会在第二句话后走神。

children.author: Melonai
children.textRawHtml:
I noticed that too, I guess the content in the training data suggests that when some text self-identifies as "cold, hard logic" it additionally assumes a mean tone. Additionally it makes responses more likely to disagree even when given inputs that are more or less valid. A while ago I tested out giving some idea to a model with the "hard logic" prompt, then taking its own output, reverting the conversation and giving it its own statement again, making it disagree with the very same statement it just made itself. This was mostly just humorous, and doesn't indicate too much, after all both statements might've been wrong in some way, but the statement itself was moreso an opinion with no fully right answer, showing this tendency to disagree.

I guess when we tell models to use "cold logic" they don't interpret that purely definitionally, but moreso with what this statement usually implies, which is often a disagreement between two people, one trying to leverage supposed logic to disagree and denigrate the other party, oftentimes actually arguing out of emotion and not the logic they claim to use. This probably occurs enough to give model responses a mean tint. That's my theory on why this could occur at least.
children.textZhHtml:
我也注意到了这一点，我猜训练数据中的内容表明，当某些文本自我识别为“冷酷、强硬的逻辑”时，它还会呈现出刻薄的语气。此外，即使给出的输入或多或少有效，它也会使响应更有可能出现不一致。不久前，我测试了用“硬逻辑”提示向模型提供一些想法，然后获取其自己的输出，恢复对话并再次给出自己的陈述，使其与刚刚自己做出的陈述不一致。这主要只是幽默，并没有说明太多，毕竟这两种陈述可能在某种程度上都是错误的，但陈述本身更多的是一种没有完全正确答案的观点，显示出这种不同意的倾向。

我想当我们告诉模型使用“冷逻辑”时，他们不会纯粹从定义上解释这一点，更重要的是这个陈述通常意味着什么，这通常是两个人之间的分歧，一个人试图利用所谓的逻辑来反对和诽谤对方，通常实际上是这样争论是出于情感，而不是他们声称使用的逻辑。这种情况的发生可能足以使模型响应呈现出平均的色调。这就是我关于为什么这种情况至少会发生的理论。

esbuild doesn't rely on TypeScript at all, so there's no issue there.

With tsdown on the other hand, it depends on if you use --isolatedDeclarations. If not, you can install TypeScript 6 side-by-side (instructions for this are on the blog)

children.textRawHtml:
Not the op, but this TS migration started long before AI was able to help. It was done slowly and carefully, as a project supporting millions of users should. And the benefits are very clear.

Bun’s port was a vibe coding fever dream that happened from one day to the next, with much looser motive, and yet to be proven reliable.
children.textZhHtml:
不是操作，但这种 TS 迁移早在 AI 能够提供帮助之前就开始了。它是缓慢而谨慎地完成的，就像一个支持数百万用户的项目应该做的那样。好处非常明显。

Bun 的移植是一个氛围编码狂热梦想，它日复一日地发生，动机更加宽松，但尚未被证明可靠。

children.author: spartanatreyu
children.textRawHtml:
> this TS migration started long before AI was able to help

Yes, but that doesn't mean it wasn't also "helped" along by AI.

See:

1. https://github.com/microsoft/typescript-go/pull/1387

2. https://github.com/microsoft/typescript-go/pull/2978

3. https://github.com/microsoft/typescript-go/pull/1138

4. etc...

Copilot is the "user" with the 2nd most commits (and the 15th and 19th too), and that's just what's been tracked in git.

---

The initial port was automated, then devs got in there, then LLMs got in there.
children.textZhHtml:
> 这种 TS 迁移早在人工智能能够提供帮助之前就开始了

是的，但这并不意味着它也没有得到人工智能的“帮助”。

参见：

1。 https://github.com/microsoft/typescript-go/pull/1387

2. https://github.com/microsoft/typescript-go/pull/2978

3. https://github.com/microsoft/typescript-go/pull/1138

4.等等...

Copilot 是提交次数第二多的“用户”（还有第 15 和第 19），这正是 git 中跟踪的内容。

---

初始端口是自动化的，然后开发人员进入那里，然后 LLM 进入那里。

children.author: docmars
children.textRawHtml:
Bun's migration to Rust was nothing more than a marketing stunt to sell more Claude subs under the impression it can perform this kind of work at scale, assuming that most who were convinced by it wouldn't look under the hood at what really took place.

It has its merits as a proof of concept that could eventually be cleaned up and released properly later, but I can't see it any other way.

Too many see it as this miraculous one-shot and are using it as a blueprint to justify more layoffs and buzzword salad in their boisterous LinkedIn announcements about how they're "completely overhauling their strategy" in engineering. Hogwash.
children.textZhHtml:
Bun 迁移到 Rust 无非是一种营销噱头，目的是为了销售更多的 Claude 潜艇，给人一种它可以大规模执行此类工作的印象，假设大多数相信它的人不会去了解到底发生了什么。

作为概念证明，它有其优点，最终可以在以后得到清理并正确发布，但我无法以任何其他方式看待它。

太多人将其视为他们在 LinkedIn 上发布了关于如何“彻底改革工程战略”的热闹公告，并以此为蓝图来证明更多裁员和流行语沙拉的合理性。废话。

children.author: jorisw
children.textRawHtml: Also it cost $165K in tokens
children.textZhHtml: 还花费了 16.5 万美元的代币

children.author: Cthulhu_
children.textRawHtml: Probably cheaper than doing it by hand, however, that's the short term "port x to y" cost, the longer term cost (or benefit) is a lot harder to calculate.
children.textZhHtml: 可能比手工操作便宜，但是，这是短期“端口 x 到 y”成本，长期成本（或收益）更难计算。

children.author: Tadpole9181
children.textRawHtml:
Not sure I understand. Bun's changes are merged on the dev branch and available for use, no?

EDIT: Oh, look, blog post on the front page now.

https://bun.com/blog/bun-in-rust

> Bun v1.3.14 was the last version of Bun written in Zig. Bun v1.4.0 will be the first version of Bun written in Rust. It's available in canary now.

So, yes, it seems it was definitely more than a "marketing stunt" and it's broadly available and slated to be the production release soon.
children.textZhHtml:
不确定我是否理解。 Bun 的更改已合并到开发分支并可供使用，不是吗？

编辑：哦，看，博客文章现在出现在首页了。

https://bun.com/blog/bun-in-rust

> Bun v1.3.14 是用 Zig 编写的 Bun 的最后一个版本。 Bun v1.4.0 将是第一个用 Rust 编写的 Bun 版本。它现在可以在金丝雀中使用。

所以，是的，它似乎绝对不仅仅是一个“营销噱头”，它已经广泛使用并且预计很快就会发布。

children.author: pjmlp
children.textRawHtml:
The irony is that the blog post actually points out as pain points the reasons many of us assert languages like Zig are out of place in the 21st century.

A nice collection of heap-use-after-free crash, use-after-free crash, crash and out-of-bounds read, memory leak, double-free crash, race condition crash.
children.textZhHtml:
讽刺的是，这篇博客文章实际上指出了我们许多人断言 Zig 这样的语言在 21 世纪不合适的原因。

堆释放后使用崩溃、释放后使用崩溃、崩溃和越界读取、内存泄漏、双释放崩溃、竞争条件崩溃的一个很好的集合。

children.author: andai
children.textRawHtml: Bun is infrastructure. Why would I want my infrastructure to be unstable? (By the way, 10,000 unsafe blocks last I checked, though the number is going down somewhat.)
children.textZhHtml: Bun 是基础设施。为什么我希望我的基础设施不稳定？ （顺便说一句，我上次检查了 10,000 个不安全的区块，尽管这个数字有所下降。）

children.author: Tadpole9181
children.textRawHtml:
I've never used bun on production for this very reason. But nevertheless, tens of thousands of people and businesses do.

And I'm not sure how you're responding to my comment. The parent said "this is a marketing stunt" derogatorily, as if it's slop that doesn't work. This is already the canary build, it's more stable than the current stable, and is actively in production products in wide use.

The parent is objectively wrong, whether or not I personally use Bun.
children.textZhHtml:
正是因为这个原因，我从未在生产环境中使用过 Bun。但尽管如此，仍有数以万计的个人和企业 做。

我真不明白你这是在怎么回复我的评论。原评论轻蔑地说“这不过是个营销噱头”，仿佛它是什么毫无用处的粗制滥造之物。这已经是金丝雀版本了，比当前的稳定版还要稳定，并且已经广泛应用于各类实际生产的产品中。

无论我个人是否使用 Bun，父级评论客观上都是错的。
